title: 28th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2012 — Écolier (Grades 3-4)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: Basil wants to write the word MATHEMATICS on a sheet of paper. He wants different letters to be coloured differently, and the same letters to be coloured identically. How many colours will he need?
questions.solution: The distinct letters in MATHEMATICS are M, A, T, H, E, I, C, S — exactly 8 different letters, so Basil needs 8 colours.
solution_steps: List unique letters: M, A, T, H, E, I, C, S.

questions.question_text: In four of the five pictures the white area is equal to the grey area. In which picture are the white area and the grey area different?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the five figures. In picture D the white and grey areas are unequal; in all others the areas are equal.
solution_steps: Examine each figure and compare white vs. grey area.
In options A, B, C, E the regions are balanced (equal area by symmetry).
In option D the areas are not equal — this is the answer.

questions.question_text: Father hangs the laundry outside on a clothesline. He wants to use as few pegs as possible. For 3 towels he needs 4 pegs. How many pegs does he need for 9 towels?
questions.solution: The first towel uses 2 pegs; each additional towel shares one peg with its neighbor and adds one new peg. So n towels need n + 1 pegs. For 9 towels: 9 + 1 = 10 pegs.
solution_steps: Check with 3 towels: 3 + 1 = 4 pegs ✓ (matches given information).

questions.question_text: Iljo colours the squares A2, B1, B2, B3, B4, C3, D3 and D4. Which colouring does he get?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1. Plotting the cells A2, B1–B4, C3, D3, D4 on a 4×4 grid produces the pattern shown in option C.
solution_steps: Mark squares A2, B1, B2, B3, B4, C3, D3, D4 on the 4-column (A–D), 4-row (1–4) grid.
Compare the resulting shape to the five options — it matches option C.

questions.question_text: 13 children are playing hide and seek. One of them is the 'seeker' and the others hide. After a while 9 children have been found. How many children are still hiding?
questions.solution: 13 children total; 1 is the seeker, so 12 are hiding. After 9 are found, 12 − 9 = 3 children are still hiding.

questions.question_text: Mike and Jake were playing darts. Each one threw three darts (see the picture). Who won and how many more points did he score?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the two dartboards showing where each player's three darts landed. Adding up scores: Mike's total exceeds Jake's by 4 points.
solution_steps: Read Mike's three dart scores from his board (PDF page 2).
Read Jake's three dart scores from his board.
Mike's total − Jake's total = 4, so Mike wins by 4 points.

questions.question_text: A regular rectangular pattern on a wall was created with 2 kinds of tiles: grey and striped. Some tiles have fallen off the wall (see the picture). How many grey tiles have fallen off?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the tile pattern. By identifying the regular grey/striped arrangement and counting the missing grey positions, 7 grey tiles have fallen off.
solution_steps: Examine the regular pattern of grey and striped tiles (PDF page 2).
Identify all empty spaces in the grid.
Determine which empty spaces should be grey based on the pattern — count gives 7.

questions.question_text: The year 2012 is a leap year, that means there are 29 days in February. Today, on the 15th March 2012, the ducklings of my grandfather are 20 days old. When did they hatch from their eggs?
questions.solution: Count back 20 days from March 15. Going back 15 days reaches March 0 = February 29 (2012 is a leap year). Going back 5 more days gives February 24.

questions.question_text: You have L-shaped tiles, each consisting of 4 squares. How many of the following shapes can you make by glueing together two of these tiles?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the four candidate shapes. All four shapes can be formed by joining two L-tetrominoes in different orientations.
solution_steps: An L-tetromino can be rotated and flipped into 4 orientations.
Test each of the four given shapes by trying to split it into two L-tetrominoes.
All four shapes are achievable → answer is 4.

questions.question_text: Three balloons cost 12 cents more than one balloon. How much does one balloon cost?
questions.solution: If one balloon costs x cents, then 3x = x + 12, so 2x = 12 and x = 6 cents.

questions.question_text: Grandmother made 20 gingerbread biscuits for her grandchildren. She decorated them with raisins and nuts. First she decorated 15 cakes with raisins and then 15 cakes with nuts. At least how many cakes were decorated both with raisins and nuts?
questions.solution: By inclusion-exclusion, |raisins ∩ nuts| ≥ |raisins| + |nuts| − total = 15 + 15 − 20 = 10.
solution_steps: At least 10 cakes had both decorations.

questions.question_text: In a sudoku the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4 can occur only once in each column and in each row. In the mathematical sudoku below Patrick first writes in the results of the calculations. Then he completes the sudoku. Which number will Patrick put in the grey cell?
questions.solution: Compute each cell: 1×1=1, 1×3=3, 2×2=4, 6−3=3, 6−5=1, 4−1=3, 1+3=4, 8−7=1, 9−7=2, 2−1=1. Filling the 4×4 sudoku grid and applying row/column uniqueness uniquely determines the grey cell to be 3.
solution_steps: Column 1 has 1,4,3,2 — all present. Column 2 needs 2 in Row1 → Row1=[1,2,?,3].
Row1 needs 4 → Row1C3=4. Row2 needs 2 → Row2C3=2. Row3 needs 2 → Row3C4=2.
Row4 needs 3,4. Column 3 has 4,2,1 → needs 3 → Row4C3=3 (grey cell). Row4C4=4.

questions.question_text: Among Nikolay's classmates there are twice as many girls as boys. Which of the following numbers can be equal to the number of all children in this class?
questions.solution: Nikolay himself is a boy not counted among 'classmates'. If there are b other boys and 2b girls among classmates, the total class size = Nikolay + b + 2b = 3b + 1. Testing 25: 25 = 3×8 + 1, so b = 8 works (8 other boys, 16 girls, plus Nikolay = 25). No other option fits 3b + 1.
solution_steps: Nikolay is not a 'classmate' of himself — total class = 1 + (classmates).
Classmates: b boys + 2b girls = 3b people → total = 3b + 1.

questions.question_text: In the animal's school, 3 kittens, 4 ducklings, 2 goslings and several lambs are taking lessons. The teacher owl found out that all of her pupils have 44 legs altogether. How many lambs are among them?
questions.solution: Kittens have 4 legs each, ducklings and goslings have 2 legs each, lambs have 4 legs each. Total without lambs: 3×4 + 4×2 + 2×2 = 12 + 8 + 4 = 24. Remaining legs: 44 − 24 = 20. Lambs: 20 ÷ 4 = 5.

questions.question_text: A cuboid is made of four pieces, as shown. Each piece consists of four cubes and is a single colour. What is the shape of the white piece?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the assembled cuboid and the five possible white-piece shapes. The white piece fits the cuboid only in the configuration shown in option D.
solution_steps: Identify the positions of the coloured pieces inside the cuboid (PDF page 3).
Determine which 4-cube region remains for the white piece.
The remaining white region matches the shape in option D.

questions.question_text: At a Christmas party there was exactly one candlestick on each of the 15 tables. There were 6 five-branched candlesticks, the rest of them were three-branched ones. How many candles had to be bought for all the candlesticks?
questions.solution: Three-branched candlesticks: 15 − 6 = 9. Total candles: 6×5 + 9×3 = 30 + 27 = 57.

questions.question_text: A grasshopper wants to climb a staircase with many steps. She makes only two different jumps: 3 steps up or 4 steps down. Beginning at the ground level, at least how many jumps will she have to make in order to take a rest on the 22nd step?
questions.solution: Let u = up-jumps (+3 each) and d = down-jumps (−4 each). We need 3u − 4d = 22, minimising u + d. Since 22 + 4d must be divisible by 3, and 22 ≡ 1 (mod 3), we need d ≡ 2 (mod 3). The smallest such d is 2: u = (22 + 8)/3 = 10. Total jumps = 10 + 2 = 12.

questions.question_text: Frank made a domino snake of seven tiles. He put the tiles next to each other so that the sides with the same number of dots were touching. Originally the snake had 33 dots on its back. However, his brother George took away two tiles from the snake. How many dots were in the place with the question mark?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the domino snake. From the original total of 33 dots and the visible dots on the remaining five tiles, the hidden value at the question mark is 4.
solution_steps: Note the original snake had 33 exposed dots across 7 tiles.
George removed 2 tiles; the remaining snake is shown in the figure (PDF page 4).
Use the matching-ends constraint to determine the unknown value: 4.

questions.question_text: Gregor forms two numbers with the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6. Both numbers have three digits, each digit is used only once. He adds these two numbers. What is the greatest sum Gregor can get?
questions.solution: Maximise the sum by placing the largest digits in the highest place values. Hundreds digits should be 6 and 5 (sum contribution 1100), tens digits 4 and 3 (sum contribution 70), units digits 2 and 1 (sum contribution 3). Maximum sum = 1173, e.g. 641 + 532 = 1173.

questions.question_text: Laura, Iggy, Val and Kate want to be in one photo together. Kate and Laura are best friends and they want to stand next to each other. Iggy wants to stand next to Laura because he likes her. In how many possible ways can they arrange for the photo?
questions.solution: Since both Kate and Iggy must be next to Laura, Laura must be in an interior position flanked by Kate and Iggy. Laura can be in position 2 or 3 in the row of four. For each position Laura has 2 arrangements of Kate/Iggy on her sides, giving 2×2 = 4 valid arrangements.
solution_steps: Laura must have Kate on one side and Iggy on the other (she can't be at an end).
Laura at position 2: K-L-I-V or I-L-K-V → 2 arrangements.
Laura at position 3: V-K-L-I or V-I-L-K → 2 arrangements.

questions.question_text: A special clock has 3 hands of different length (for hours, for minutes, and for seconds). We do not know which hand is which, but we know that the clock runs correctly. At 12:55:30 p.m. the hands were in position depicted on the right. How will this clock look like at 8:11:00 p.m.?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5. At 12:55:30 the three hands are identified by their positions (hour near 12, minute near 11, second at 6). At 8:11:00 the hour hand is near 8, the minute hand at the 2-minute mark, and the second hand at 12 — matching option E.
solution_steps: At 12:55:30: hour hand ≈ 27.75° from 12; minute hand at 330° (near 11); second hand at 180° (at 6).
Use the figure (PDF page 5) to identify which drawn hand is which by their lengths.
At 8:11:00: hour hand ≈ 245.5°; minute hand at 66°; second hand at 0° (12).
The configuration matching these positions is option E.

questions.question_text: Michael chose some positive number, multiplied it by itself, added 1, multiplied the result by 10, added 3, and multiplied the result by 4. His final answer was 2012. What number did Michael choose?

A rectangular paper sheet measures 192 × 84 mm. You cut the sheet along just one straight line to get two parts, one of which is a square. Then you do the same with the non-square part of the sheet, and so on. What is the length of the side of the smallest square you can get with this procedure?
questions.solution: This process is the geometric version of the Euclidean algorithm, ending at GCD(192, 84). GCD(192, 84): 192 = 2×84 + 24; 84 = 3×24 + 12; 24 = 2×12 + 0. GCD = 12 mm.
solution_steps: 24 × 12: cut two 12×12 squares — smallest square side = 12 mm.

questions.question_text: In a soccer game the winner gains 3 points, while the loser gains 0 points. If the game is a draw, then the two teams gain 1 point each. A team has played 38 games gaining 80 points. Find the greatest possible number of games that the team lost.
questions.solution: Let w = wins, d = draws, l = losses. Then w + d + l = 38 and 3w + d = 80. Solving: d = 80 − 3w and l = 2w − 42. To maximise l, maximise w. Constraint d ≥ 0 gives w ≤ 26. At w = 26: d = 2, l = 10. Verify: 26 + 2 + 10 = 38 ✓, 78 + 2 = 80 ✓.
solution_steps: Subtract first from second: 2w − l = 42 → l = 2w − 42.
Maximise l by maximising w. Need d = 80 − 3w ≥ 0 → w ≤ 26.
